text:
Modern high-performance object detectors increasingly rely on detection proposals to narrow the search space, replacing traditional exhaustive sliding window techniques. However, despite their integration into state-of-the-art frameworks, the specific trade-offs inherent in different proposal generation methods remain poorly understood. This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of twelve prominent proposal methods and four baselines, evaluating them across several critical dimensions: repeatability, ground truth recall on PASCAL, ImageNet, and MS COCO datasets, and their downstream impact on DPM, R-CNN, and Fast R-CNN detector performance.

Our empirical findings demonstrate that for effective object detection, improving the localization accuracy of a proposal is just as vital as maximizing high recall. To capture this relationship, we introduce Average Recall (AR), a novel metric that simultaneously rewards both coverage and spatial precision. We show that AR correlates remarkably well with final detector accuracy, far outperforming traditional metrics like recall at a single overlap threshold. By identifying the systematic strengths and weaknesses of existing approaches, this work provides a unified framework and actionable insights for practitioners to select and fine-tune proposal methods for a variety of computer vision tasks.
